# 📝 Notes

Apr 29, 2026

## 8am AI\!

Invited [David Olsson](mailto:david@atomic47.co) [eric@scroll.ca](mailto:eric@scroll.ca) [giroday@gmail.com](mailto:giroday@gmail.com) [hanif.janmohamed@gmail.com](mailto:hanif.janmohamed@gmail.com) [Fulvio Ciano](mailto:fulvio@atomic47.co) [scott@driftresourcetechnologies.com](mailto:scott@driftresourcetechnologies.com) [Jason Kryski](mailto:jason@mttr.ca) [Jen Boger](mailto:jen@atomic47.co) [Shabraj Shahid](mailto:shabraj@mttr.ca) [spencer@ratiomachina.com](mailto:spencer@ratiomachina.com) [graham@ratiomachina.com](mailto:graham@ratiomachina.com) [Bailey Klinger](mailto:bwklinger@gmail.com) [andre.bertram3@gmail.com](mailto:andre.bertram3@gmail.com) [mike@smartdeskcrm.com](mailto:mike@smartdeskcrm.com) [Spencer MacBeth](mailto:spencer@mttr.ca)

Attachments [8am AI\!](https://calendar.google.com/calendar/event?eid=aW52N2w4MWJvNjJqYm5zYmQxZTMzYjllNmZfMjAyNjA0MjlUMTUwMDAwWiBkYXZpZEBhdG9taWM0Ny5jbw)

Meeting records [Transcript](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1w7EVat2mgyggbE2dboQ0-Wm8JckiVF_I0MaD9fUkR7M/edit?usp=drive_web&tab=t.skfsc0uskz5t) 

### Summary

Teams discussed operational shifts via Large Language Models and necessity for strategic leadership amidst changing roles.

**LLM Impact on Rigor**  
Teams observed that Large Language Models enable superficial updates, necessitating increased one-on-one sessions to ensure genuine cognitive engagement. Developers now act as auditors to maintain quality standards.

**Strategic Planning Shift**  
Meetings transitioned from status reporting to rigorous planning where developers present sound strategies for critique. Automation now provides transparency for executives, removing the need for manual progress tracking.

**Redefining Team Paradigms**  
Specialized roles are normalizing toward generalist contributions, creating management overhead. Leadership must mandate clear processes and learning loops to help teams adapt to this new paradigm.

*Rate this Summary:* [Helpful](https://google.qualtrics.com/jfe/form/SV_4YkxrBAaiTVqYCi?isGoogler=false&isHelpful=true) or [Not Helpful](https://google.qualtrics.com/jfe/form/SV_4YkxrBAaiTVqYCi?isGoogler=false&isHelpful=false)

### Decisions

We've **updated the Decisions section** using your feedback.

Let us know what you think: [Helpful](https://bit.ly/4j0NRI9) or [Not Helpful](https://bit.ly/4p4GaCr)

## ALIGNED

* **Meeting focus shifted to planning** The team established a new meeting protocol that focuses on planning and resolving ambiguity rather than reporting project status.

* **Leadership prioritized over individual preference** The group decided to prioritize strong, centralized leadership direction over individual team member preferences regarding workflow and tool adoption.

### Next steps

- [ ] \[Juan\] Assess Career: Explore opportunities for higher salary or potential consultancy work.

### Details

*Did the screenshots in this section make your notes [better](https://goo.gle/44IH7J9) or [worse](https://goo.gle/4b5ZWtJ)?*

* **Challenges in Organizational Partnerships**: David Olsson discussed the precarious position of a consultant when partnering with larger organizations, noting that the partner's ability to operationalize and consume what is offered can quickly usurp or absorb the consultant's role. They highlighted that sometimes when a consultant offers to do work, the partner simply learns what needs to be done and assigns the tasks internally, which underscores the challenge of maintaining authority in such partnerships ([00:02:58](#00:02:58)).

* **Team Evolution and Learning Gaps**: David Olsson observed that Juan's team is evolving, potentially unknowingly, as Juan helps them, but they are also recognizing a learning loop where team members are focused only on getting work done rather than developing a deeper understanding ([00:02:58](#00:02:58)). Juan noted that they had increased individual meetings to every two days with each team member because, while the LLM tools enable them to give eloquent standup updates, sitting down individually helps uncover what is actually missing in their understanding and work process ([00:04:36](#00:04:36)).

* **Impact of LLMs on Work Rigor**: Juan explained that team members are using LLMs to "cheat" by prompting the tool, pushing the output, and using the fluent result to provide updates that make it seem as though they did the underlying work. David Olsson acknowledged this behavior, noting that programmers have long favored the fastest way to get something done, even if it is not the best, comparing it to the real-world issue of cheating on homework ([00:06:21](#00:06:21)).

* **Difficulty in Collaborative Feedback Loops**: David Olsson described the difficulty in working with feedback and comments in the current LLM workflow, where criticisms like "this needs to change" do not always provide enough context for effective iteration ([00:07:16](#00:07:16)). They suggested that the fluency provided by LLM tools can obscure true productivity, making the feedback loop interesting but not always useful because the individual providing the work might simply throw the comments back at the LLM to fix without internalizing the necessary rigor ([00:08:21](#00:08:21)).

* **Shifting Focus to Cognitive Effort and Value in Work**: David Olsson noted that Juan is addressing the quality problem by spending time in one-on-one sessions to work through the cognitive effort that went into producing assets, thereby determining where the person is truly engaged with the problem. Juan confirmed their approach is to remove ambiguity, with the product manager creating better specs and tickets using LLM-powered skills and agents to define the developer's required actions ([00:09:29](#00:09:29)).

* **Using Skills and Agents to Structure Development Work**: Juan described how a specialized "skill" now mandates that developers first check code location, architecture, and infrastructure before creating a plan, which helps remove ambiguity from tickets. This skill also logs changes to the codebase (reference) and structural decisions (engineering), allowing Juan to review the engineering decisions and changes made to the architecture or model ([00:10:40](#00:10:40)). Juan also noted that they must consistently retrain developers on the process because one developer, for instance, finished a feature but failed to execute the final "push and create the pull request" command, indicating a lack of full awareness of the automated process ([00:11:50](#00:11:50)).

* **The Challenge of Oversimplified Tasks in LLM Environment**: Juan explained that LLMs reduce the difficulty and friction for tackling any type of ticket, resulting in developers attempting tasks outside their expertise, such as infrastructure changes, because they "feel that it's easy". Juan is trying to manage this by structuring tasks so they view developers primarily as automators or auditors who check the ticket, process, and output, and then discuss the output before pushing the work ([00:13:28](#00:13:28)). David Olsson agreed that this concept of validating work is a crucial step they have automated, though they noted the automation does not always provide feedback necessary to help people get better at their jobs ([00:14:36](#00:14:36)).

* **Shifting Meeting Focus from Status to Planning**: David Olsson noted that their own project has automated all reporting, making status meetings unnecessary, leading to a major shift where meetings are now focused on problem-solving or planning where the team is going, rather than reviewing what has been done ([00:24:48](#00:24:48)). Juan agreed with this shift, explaining that their meetings are now focused on planning, where developers bring their plans, Juan "pokes holes" in the plan, and only when the plan is sound is the developer allowed to execute it using the LLM tools ([00:23:44](#00:23:44)).

* **New Paradigm and the Need for Broader Context**: Fulvio Ciano asserted that the old work model depended on compartmentalized knowledge and ticket systems, but the new paradigm requires sharing the entire process. They emphasized that it is now relevant to understand how a ticket fits into the "greater goals and the shared strategies," requiring visibility on the whole context, not just little deliverables and their connections ([00:25:45](#00:25:45)). David Olsson supported this, noting that technology allows for easier transparency, such as generating reports from ticketing systems that executives want to see, which used to be multiple people's jobs ([00:28:48](#00:28:48)).

  ![][image1]

* **Impact of New Tools on Team Structure and Roles**: Jason Kryski reflected that the "typical agile structure" is not working for their four-person team, noting they are probably operating at only 70% of their capability despite moving quickly, leading them to consider rebuilding their system based on foundational principles ([00:30:44](#00:30:44)). Fulvio Ciano observed that everyone appears to be normalizing to a high level of understanding of the whole, rather than their specialized piece, which is challenging for people trained in the previous paradigm that relied on compartmentalized roles (e.g., PM, QA, UX/UI) ([00:32:52](#00:32:52)).

* **Experience, Pattern Recognition, and the "Solenoiod" Analogy**: Juan shared that their experience across various architectures and technologies gives them an advantage, referencing a movie analogy where someone with experience knows where the "solenoid" is and why it is failing, unlike those without that breadth of knowledge ([00:35:19](#00:35:19)). Juan stated that they are now focusing on mentoring their team to understand more and think more deeply so their conversations can improve ([00:36:28](#00:36:28)).

* **The Challenge of Upskilling and Transparency in the New Era**: Juan argued that while people have always had to go and learn new tools and technology, the current environment demands skills not traditionally taught in computer science (e.g., planning, communication), raising the barrier for individuals to learn the base principles ([00:37:27](#00:37:27)). Juan added that the high transparency of the new tools makes it clear when someone is not studying or grasping the whole idea, making the lack of improvement very visible ([00:38:46](#00:38:46)).

* **The "Muddle" of Roles and Loss of Specialization**: Hanif Janmohamed described the current situation as "normalizing to the muddle," where everyone can do a bit of everything, which makes it hard to identify their own "wheelhouse" and where they can genuinely make an additive contribution ([00:38:46](#00:38:46)). They noted that this requires "a lot of altitude" to understand where strengths and capacities lie ([00:41:21](#00:41:21)).

* **Increased Expectations and Management Overhead**: Jason Kryski expressed concern that expectations have rapidly changed and grown due to the technology's enablement, creating management overhead as leaders must constantly redefine roles that were previously static ([00:41:21](#00:41:21)). David Olsson agreed that output is now so easy that fluency can obscure the need for deeper analysis, and they reflected on how their own role has shifted from focusing on functions to orchestrating a whole team process ([00:43:01](#00:43:01)).

* **The Need for a Learning Loop for the Human in the Loop**: David Olsson noted that the fluency offered by the tools easily overachieves past challenges, making it difficult for people to know if they have truly done their job well, unlike the wisdom gained from great pain and repeated effort in the past ([00:45:14](#00:45:14)). They stressed the need to provide the "human in the loop" with a learning loop that offers a viable career path, recognizing that the current challenge is bringing in people to learn new patterns that change monthly when those learning loops are not on rails ([00:47:30](#00:47:30)).

* **The Call for Leadership and Clear Direction**: Jason Kryski concluded that, given the varying adoption and perspectives on the new tools, the current period demands strong leadership to clearly define "this is how we're doing this" and "this is how we're thinking about this" ([00:51:13](#00:51:13)). Fulvio Ciano concurred, stating this is not a time for preference but for leadership ([00:52:18](#00:52:18)).

* **Personal Reflection and Consulting Opportunities**: Juan shared their personal feeling that they are on the right track and might be able to help a bigger team, suggesting the possibility of consultancy in this area. David Olsson affirmed Juan's value as a leader in upskilling teams and cautioned them that if they consult, they should ensure the focus is "really pointed" and charge a significant amount from the start ([00:53:31](#00:53:31)).

*You should review Gemini's notes to make sure they're accurate. [Get tips and learn how Gemini takes notes](https://support.google.com/meet/answer/14754931)*

*How is the quality of **these specific notes?** [Take a short survey](https://google.qualtrics.com/jfe/form/SV_9vK3UZEaIQKKE7A?confid=ZHbhKrnALSa9sqfJV21tDxITOAIIigIgABgBCA&detailid=standard&screenshot=true) to let us know your feedback, including how helpful the notes were for your needs.*

**Juan:** So for with that one, we we reduce the the ambiguity of what the developer has to do, right? because like you said before it was a two sentence and then two sentences that are inter that are read by the LLM and then whatever he can come up with with the with the search. But now if we have a better prompt or a better ticket then that combined with the skill that says okay when you have a ticket the first thing that you need to do is check where the code is. this is the architecture, this is the infrastructure, this is all the things and layers take into account that give me a plan and then the the same skill has that during the conversation as the ticket progresses it logs uh it divides into two different camps what it is reference which means changes to the code base and what I call engineering and is decisions that have been made around the ticket that change things in the structure. So that way I can see okay you took the ticket you solved it this way and this is the things you change in in our architecture or in in our model.

So my goal has been to try to make it better noting that we're building wikis and and and to a certain extent the project state itself it is meant for the AEI to enable us but I'm really trying to appreciate what it means to upskill a team in a project in an evolutionary way to create that formative context that can reside at the level of a project or initiative and be shared amongst a team.

**David Olsson:** And so knowing the ins and outs and which one you've been working with a number of developers and your team to be able to optimize them to work within this new sort of paradigm, it helps any organization recognize that a lot of the time they're being additive to each other and is the additive really what their value is. And uh and so I point to these tools um as a way of trying to appreciate what it means to have a better feedback loop. **David Olsson:** So this stuff of figuring out how people work and how you document things and how you create intelligence over top of it is enabling us to figure out what it means to have an intelligence cluster, what it means to have a server nowadays and what it means to run software um in the cloud and on prem because I think that's how everything will work in the future. I'm about to approach other parts of our company with the same strategy in mind and and already from the discussion this morning and thinking about things over the last few days, the human in the loop is not considered enough in my work.

**David Olsson:** So, I don't know what they're doing.

You bring me your plan,

**David Olsson:** Awesome.

**Juan Rojas:** I'll poke holes into your plan, and when I know that the plan is sound and you understand what the plan means, then I I'm I'm fine with you going and running away with the LLM because I know that that toy factory and all the toys are there to control that flow. But the biggest thing for me right now is is making the developer understand what we're doing and why are we doing it.

### 00:24:48 {#00:24:48}

**Juan Rojas:** That's that's so big.

**David Olsson:** I like that shift. Um, so a lot of meetings are about status and where people have gotten things done. And that's the one thing with the last version of the project state that pushed me to publish it is that on our project where we're working um within the protein industry um I was able to to push all the reporting off of people's plates. And so why have a meeting if you're just going to report what you did? And so the meetings are now about like okay we don't know what to do or this is a problem or let's do the other thing and set up the rest of the week like you say it moves from a a retrospective of what has been done to where are you going and um that's a big shift. ### 00:25:45 {#00:25:45}

**David Olsson:** good.

The old model was very much depending on you know what tools you used canban water flow or or or um charts or agile. It was very much about compartmentalized and people doing a little piece, right? And so you only needed to have like knowledge of what piece was required before your piece and what piece your piece contributed to, right? Like like it was it was hugely about compartmentalized knowledge. And so and that led to this whole ticket system, right? A piece corresponded to a ticket. You gave a ticket out to a person depending on a bunch of other, you know, targeting factors. But now like that's irrelevant. Um what's relevant is like being able to share the process because like like not just knowing your piece, not just knowing like uh this more detail about the ticket, but how the ticket fits into the greater goals and the greater goals and the shared the shared uh strategies, right?
